title: ENMOkit
tagline: Computes ENMO, MAD and intensity-gradient metrics from raw acceleration at configurable epochs.
author: P. Andersson
description: Reimplementing standard metrics leads to subtle, study-breaking differences. After QC, to derive comparable movement metrics. Outputs: Epoch-level ENMO/MAD time series + daily summaries.

title: PolicySim
tagline: Simulates population health impact of activity-shift scenarios.
author: H. Bauer
description: Policy teams need scenario estimates, not just associations. For "what if the population moved more" projections. Outputs: Projected outcome shifts under scenarios.

title: epochr
tagline: Flexible re-epoching and cut-point application across multiple metric definitions.
author: J. Lindqvist
description: Comparing cut-points means rerunning whole pipelines. During sensitivity analysis of intensity thresholds. Outputs: Activity intensity classifications per epoch.

title: wearQC
tagline: Automated quality-control flags for non-wear, calibration drift and clipping in free-living data.
author: S. Novak
description: Unflagged poor-quality segments bias downstream metrics. Immediately after ingestion, before deriving any activity metrics. Outputs: Per-window quality flags + a participant-level QC report.

title: DeviceSync
tagline: Aligns multi-sensor wearable streams (accelerometer, PPG, GPS) to a shared clock.
author: A. Kovač
description: Sensors drift and start at different times, breaking fusion. When combining two or more devices per participant. Outputs: Time-aligned long-format dataset with offset diagnostics.

title: ActiPlot
tagline: Publication-ready actigraphy heatmaps and 24-hour radial plots.
author: F. Klein
description: Raw plots rarely meet publication standards. When preparing figures for papers or reports. Outputs: High-resolution figures + reusable plot configs.

title: Translating accelerometer evidence into public-health guidance
tagline: A pathway from device-measured evidence to actionable recommendations.
author: Bauer et al., 2024
description: Use the pathway to structure an evidence-to-guidance project. Keeps recommendations traceable to underlying data. For example: Framed a national activity guideline update. Limitations: Process-focused; not a substitute for expert panels.

title: Best practices for free-living accelerometer data collection
tagline: A practical checklist for placement, wear protocols and metadata capture in field studies.
author: Haugen et al., 2024
description: Adopt the checklist when writing your data-collection protocol. Reduces avoidable data loss and harmonises studies across the network. For example: Used to standardise wear protocols across three cohort sites. Limitations: Focused on wrist and thigh placement; hip guidance is lighter.

title: From data to policy: a framework for movement behaviour recommendations
tagline: Connects analysis outputs to the needs of decision-makers.
author: Bauer & Sato, 2023
description: Align outputs to decision questions early. Reduces the research-to-policy translation gap. For example: Used to brief a municipal health team. Limitations: Assumes some analytic capacity in the audience.

title: Defining behavioural archetypes from wearable data
tagline: Practical steps and pitfalls when labelling movement archetypes.
author: Ahmed & Costa, 2022
description: Follow the validation steps before naming clusters. Encourages reproducible, interpretable phenotypes. For example: Used to standardise archetype reporting in two studies. Limitations: Cluster counts remain somewhat data-driven.

title: Mendelian randomisation with device-measured physical activity
tagline: Using genetic instruments to probe causality in activity–health links.
author: Romano, 2023
description: Check instrument validity before applying MR. Offers a complementary route around confounding. For example: Applied to activity and cardiometabolic outcomes. Limitations: Requires large genotyped samples and valid instruments.

title: Movement phenotypes across the life course: a scoping review
tagline: Surveys how behavioural phenotypes are defined and used across ages.
author: Costa et al., 2024
description: Position your phenotyping approach against existing definitions. Prevents reinventing incompatible phenotype schemes. For example: Framed phenotype definitions for a youth activity study. Limitations: Coverage is lighter for clinical populations.
